title: Proposal Presentation Framework & Seven Methods for Attracting Better Clients
summary: Erin Stetzer walked participants through her seven core methods for attracting and serving ideal clients, including leading with expertise, showing how you think, maintaining standards, and delivering consistency. She then presented an actual client proposal booklet used by Erin Stetzer Homes, walking through its structure covering company overview, team, systems, scheduling, budgeting, and references. The session opened into Q&A covering topics such as pre-construction agreements, schedule hold deposits, marketing strategies, social media, and the transition from builder to project manager.

teachings.explanation: The very first instinct in any client interaction should be to identify how you can help them, even if that means directing them to someone else. This approach builds trust and positions you as a genuine advocate rather than a salesperson.
teachings.quote: Whenever a phone call comes in, or whenever I run into someone and have this conversation about working together, it's always about serving first, period, like serve first.

teachings.explanation: Being someone others genuinely want to be around requires consistent energy and care for people. This magnetism is not a tactic but a way of operating all day, every day, and it naturally attracts the right clients and partners.
teachings.quote: We want to be magnetic. We want to be someone that other people like to be around. That requires energy. And that requires caring, right? Caring about other people all day long until you go to sleep.

teachings.explanation: Builders should position themselves as knowledgeable guides, not salespeople. Sharing expert knowledge to improve someone else's situation is leadership, not selling. The role is to be a trusted guide, not the hero of the story.
teachings.quote: It's not a salesy thing. It's not a sales tactic. It's literally leading with your expert knowledge, your ownership of this knowledge. You're wanting to improve a situation for somebody else.

teachings.explanation: Sharing your actual thought process and perspective in conversations—whether at school drop-off, industry events, or professional meetings—differentiates you and builds authentic connections that lead to projects. Most people never share how they truly think, and doing so is a powerful differentiator.
teachings.quote: Those are the breadcrumbs right in front of us. We all have breadcrumbs literally in front of us. And those of us that actually pick those breadcrumbs up win.

teachings.explanation: When formally presenting to potential clients, the feeling of organization is what prospects are evaluating because they are about to spend significant money. A well-packaged, structured proposal signals that the builder is in control and calm, which directly reduces client anxiety.
teachings.quote: The number one thing that I've seen people pick up on is organization. They want to feel how organized you are, right? That is a feeling that they are looking for because they're about to spend a whole bunch of money with you.

teachings.explanation: Working with high-quality architects, designers, realtors, and vendors elevates your own standards and reputation. Refusing to work with subpar companies protects your brand and attracts better clients. These partnerships are also a key element of a credible proposal.
teachings.quote: Solid partnerships with architects, with designers, with real estate agents. Work with great companies. Don't work with junkie companies. Don't accept lower standards.

teachings.explanation: Standards cannot be effectively imposed on others; they must first be held internally. When you operate at a high standard yourself, people who share those values are naturally attracted to your company and your clients reflect those standards back.
teachings.quote: Standards are an internal game. And they're, you can judge the standards and you can get better at the standards. And then those who respect and want to be around those standards are attracted to that company or that client.

teachings.explanation: Continuously seeking growth by surrounding yourself with people you admire, removing toxic relationships, and pursuing personal challenges is how you become the kind of builder who attracts better clients and bigger projects. Pay attention to the internal nudges that push you toward something greater.
teachings.quote: Upgrade your identity, hang out with the people we respect, talk to the people we admire, reach out to those who do better than us, learn.

teachings.explanation: Being a reliable partner who shows up the same way every time—for clients, trades, and partners—is the foundational operating system. Consistency builds trust over time and is the cumulative result of all the other methods working together.
teachings.quote: Deliver consistently to people, like be the person that shows up, be the partner that serves, be the person others can rely on over and over and over and over again. And that is the operating system.

teachings.concept: Proposal as Organized Presentation of Systems
teachings.explanation: A professional proposal booklet should walk clients through who you are, your team, your communication systems, scheduling processes, weekly reporting cadence, budget management approach, and professional references. Presenting all three key areas—time, people, and money—in an organized way gives clients the confidence to say yes.
teachings.quote: You better believe people want to know that there's some organization around time, people, and money. Those three things.

teachings.concept: Qualifying Clients During the House Walk
teachings.explanation: Meeting potential clients at an active construction site serves a dual purpose: it lets them evaluate your work and lets you evaluate whether they care about the details you care about. The decision to hand them a proposal booklet at the end of the walk is contingent on whether you sense a good fit.
teachings.quote: I put this somewhere in the house where if I think they're a good fit, I'll hand it to them. If I don't think we're a great fit, I won't even hand it to them just because we're done.

actionable_advice: Take 30 minutes to an hour of quiet time after this session to write out your own personal methodology using these seven methods as a starting point.

Create a proposal booklet using a tool like Canva that includes a table of contents, company overview, team bios, communication systems, scheduling snapshots, weekly report format, budget tracking visuals, client references, and professional references.
Print the budget as a separate PDF from the main proposal booklet since budget numbers often change right before a presentation.
Print just enough proposal copies—do not over-print, as copies can end up in competitors' hands through shared architect and designer contacts.
Before walking a potential client through a house, call your project manager and all trades on site to ensure no music is playing and the house is clean and controlled.
Consider sending potential clients to walk an active construction site on their own first, without you, so they can see the real job site conditions before you meet with them.
After a house walk, call the client a few days later and ask directly whether they are 99% sure you are a good fit for them before committing to a full pricing exercise.

Explore breaking pre-construction deliverables into paid milestones—such as reviewing preliminary plans or preparing a preliminary budget—to protect your time and reduce exposure if a client exits early.
Use Loom to record your internal systems and processes, then load those recordings into ChatGPT to have the systems refined and documented more quickly.

Introduce the project manager to clients only after the pricing presentation, signed contract, and initial onboarding, and ease clients into understanding that the project manager is their primary point of contact going forward.
Post your Instagram handle in the program's community chat so other members can follow each other's builder profiles for inspiration and mutual visibility.
To build early brand awareness without a marketing budget, visit showrooms, design centers, and vendor offices to learn and form relationships—this creates word-of-mouth referrals organically.
client_challenges: David asked how and when to get a signed construction contract in a long pre-construction sales cycle where clients can drop out months in, leaving the builder with opportunity cost and no recourse.

David raised the challenge of clients walking away after months of hourly pre-construction work when the project becomes too expensive or complicated, with the builder having little financial protection beyond their hourly rate.
David asked whether a non-refundable schedule hold deposit of $25,000 to $50,000 is a viable intermediate step between pre-construction work and a signed construction contract.
A participant asked what stage of construction the house walk should be at and whether it is better to invite clients before or after finishing the home.
Renee asked how Erin's sales and presentation process worked when she was earlier in her career and her portfolio was less established.
Renee asked what specific early marketing tactics were most instrumental in building professional relationships and creating a referral network when first starting out.
John asked about Erin's philosophy on brand sponsorships and whether sponsored content conflicts with authentic marketing positioning.
A participant asked at what point in the client relationship the project manager is introduced and how the transition away from the owner as primary contact is managed.
notable_quotes: On breadcrumbs and missed opportunities: 'Those are the breadcrumbs right in front of us. We all have breadcrumbs literally in front of us. And those of us that actually pick those breadcrumbs up win. Those of us who just stomp on them and don't pay attention to it, we lose.'

On standards being internal: 'To impose that on others, when we have a larger team, I think I learned a while ago that when I expect things of people, it doesn't really work out. Expect things of ourselves. And those who operate in that fashion are attracted to that system.'

On early marketing through curiosity: 'I would hop into an engineer's office. I would hop into Ann Sachs—show me behind the scenes. That is all marketing. They get to know you. I didn't know that that was marketing. That was built out of curiosity and pure effort.'
On how the world of marketing is shifting: 'It used to be it's who you know. Now it's who knows you.'

On delivering consistency: 'Be the person that shows up, be the partner that serves, be the person others can rely on over and over and over and over again. And that is the operating system. Deliver consistency consistently.'
On being the guide, not the hero: 'That doesn't mean be the hero. That means be a guide, be a trusted advocate for someone.'
